q: Your rate-limit-by-key policy uses counter-key="constant". What behavior results?
o: All callers share one global bucket and starve each other
e: The counter key defines the bucket; a constant means every caller shares it. Use expressions like context.Request.IpAddress or a JWT claim.

q: After importing an API, calls return 401 despite a valid Entra token. The validate-jwt audience is set to the CLIENT app ID. Fix?
o: Set audience to the API's identifier (aud claim of the token)
e: aud must match the API/resource the token was issued FOR, not the client that requested it — a classic misconfiguration.

q: A daemon requests scope=api://orders/Orders.Read with client_credentials and fails. Why?
o: App-only flows must request the /.default scope; named scopes are for delegated flows
e: Client credentials uses api://orders/.default, which rolls up granted application permissions (app roles).

q: Processing takes 90 s but the queue lock duration is 30 s with no renewal. Result?
o: Message unlocks mid-processing and is redelivered — duplicates
e: Expired locks redeliver messages; renew locks or extend duration, and make handlers idempotent.

q: A queue has TTL 5 minutes and dead-lettering-on-expiration disabled. Backlogged messages…
e: Without dead-letter-on-expiration, expired messages are simply dropped — enable it so nothing vanishes unnoticed.

q: Sending to a session-enabled queue fails with 'session identifier missing'. Why?
o: Session-enabled entities require every message to carry a SessionId
e: Session queues enforce SessionId on send; receivers accept sessions rather than plain messages.

q: A VM's managed identity gets 403 reading blobs despite a 'Reader' role on the storage account. Root cause?
o: Reader is control-plane only; data access needs Storage Blob Data Reader
e: Control-plane Reader sees the resource's configuration, not blob contents — data-plane roles (DataActions) are required.

q: Your IMDS token request uses resource=https://management.azure.com/ but you call blob storage. Result?
e: Tokens are audience-bound; the storage data plane rejects management-plane tokens.

q: After creating a private endpoint, a spoke VM still resolves the storage FQDN to a public IP. Least likely cause?
o: Private DNS zone not linked to the VNet
Missing DNS zone group (no A record)
e: NSGs affect connectivity, not name resolution. The other three are the classic private-endpoint DNS failure modes.

q: Spoke A cannot reach spoke B though both peer with the hub. Peering is healthy. What is missing?
o: UDRs sending spoke traffic to the hub firewall (peering is non-transitive)
e: Transitivity must be built: route spoke↔spoke traffic through an NVA/firewall in the hub with UDRs (or use direct peering/VWAN).

q: In the hub-spoke SaaS design, why does Azure Policy deny public network access on PaaS resources when private endpoints already exist?
o: Defense in depth: architecture provides the path, policy prevents drift back to public exposure
e: Governance enforces the architecture over time — someone will eventually click 'enable public access' without it.

q: Canary traffic weights are configured but 100% of traffic hits the old revision. Likely cause?
o: App is in single-revision mode — weights need multiple-revision mode
e: Single revision mode always routes to the latest revision; traffic splitting requires multiple revision mode.

q: A KEDA Service Bus scale rule never scales the worker. Which is NOT a plausible cause?
o: Invalid connection secret for the scaler
e: Dapr is unrelated to KEDA scale rules; the others are the usual suspects.

q: jwt.ms shows your token has scp=User.Read but your API checks roles. What happened?
o: You obtained a delegated token; the API expects app roles (application permissions)
e: scp = delegated scopes (user flows); roles = app roles. Match the flow to what the API authorizes on.

q: Why are two cloud-only break-glass accounts excluded from Conditional Access?
o: So a CA misconfiguration or MFA outage cannot lock every admin out of the tenant
e: Emergency access accounts guarantee tenant access during CA/MFA failures — monitored and alerted on every use.

q: FinSecure chose password hash sync over ADFS mainly because…
o: Cloud auth keeps working during on-prem outages with near-zero infrastructure
e: PHS is the simplest, most resilient hybrid identity option — federation adds servers, certificates, and failure modes.

q: ShopSphere keeps EU order data on EU SQL servers and pairs failover groups within the same geography because…
o: GDPR data residency must survive DR — failing over to a US region would violate it
e: Residency by architecture: DR topology must respect the same data-boundary constraints as production.

q: Which Cosmos consistency did the e-commerce scenario choose for carts, and why?
o: Session — shoppers see their own cart instantly without global write latency
e: Session gives read-your-own-writes for each user at low latency — carts don't need global strong consistency.

q: ForgeWorks runs Region B as a scaled-down warm standby instead of cold IaC rebuild because…
o: 15-minute RTO can't wait for full provisioning; warm standby scales out in minutes
e: Standby temperature follows RTO: cold = hours (cheapest), warm = minutes, hot/active = seconds (priciest).

q: Why does ransomware defeat replication-only DR strategies?
o: Replication faithfully copies the encrypted/corrupted data; only point-in-time immutable backups recover
e: Replication has no memory; backup provides history. Immutability + MUA protect that history from tampering.

q: Azure SQL PITR restores go to…
o: A new database — the app must cut over or you swap names
e: PITR creates a new database on the same server; plan the rename/cutover step in your runbook and measure its time.

q: Soft delete was enabled the day AFTER a blob was deleted. Can it be recovered?
o: No — protection features only cover events after they're enabled
e: Enable soft delete/versioning/immutability BEFORE you need them; retention must also exceed detection time.

q: An SRE must trace one failed order across APIM → ACA API → Service Bus → worker. Fastest tool?
o: App Insights end-to-end transaction view (operation ID correlation)
e: Distributed tracing correlates every hop under one operation ID — the Application Map/transaction view shows the failing link.

q: In the GitOps AKS design, rollback of a bad deployment is performed by…
o: kubectl apply of old YAML from a laptop
Reverting the Git commit — Flux reconciles the cluster to the previous state
e: Git is the source of truth; the revert is audited, repeatable, and automatic.

q: Why does the deployment stack's denyWriteAndDelete matter in the IaC lab?
o: Blocks out-of-band portal edits so prod can't drift from the template
e: Deny settings enforce that changes flow through the pipeline — drift prevention, not just detection.

q: The retailer taints its Spot node pool so only batch workloads schedule there because…
o: Spot VMs can be evicted anytime; latency-critical pods must not land on them
e: Taints/tolerations keep interruptible capacity for interruptible work — cost optimization without risking SLOs.

q: 'Minimize administrative effort' for hybrid sign-in with cloud resilience. Best fit:
e: PHS = no extra servers and works during on-prem outages — the 'minimize effort' phrasing points straight to it.

q: A case study says spoke-per-customer with central egress inspection. Which two limits should the architect plan for first?
o: IP address plan (supernet) and firewall SNAT/peering scale limits
e: Hub-spoke at 50+ spokes hits addressing and hub-throughput/SNAT limits before anything else — plan the /16 on day one.

q: Which statement about test failovers is the exam-correct posture?
o: Run regularly into isolated networks; an untested DR plan doesn't count as a plan
e: ASR test failovers validate RTO without touching production — auditors and the exam both expect scheduled drills.

q: An architect must justify APIM Premium over Standard v2 in a design review. Which requirement clinches it?
o: Multi-region active gateways with internal VNet injection
e: Features common to all tiers never justify Premium; multi-region + VNet injection are Premium differentiators.
